name: /TMP/FILES
summary: Temporary anonymous upload service with 100 MB files, automatic deletion after 60 minutes, and a simple upload API.

free_model.notes: This published host has a verified free, guest, or free-account path in the dataset. Recheck first-party sources if the service moves to trial-only or paid-only access.

max_file_size.notes: The upload page says the maximum file size is 100 MB.

retention.notes: All uploaded files are automatically deleted after 60 minutes.

storage.notes: This is a temporary upload-and-share service, not a persistent personal storage plan.

bandwidth.notes: Bandwidth is conditional. The service does not publish a numeric throughput cap, and effective transfer availability is bounded by the fixed 60-minute lifetime of each upload.

account.benefits: No account or login flow is described; uploads are anonymous.

developer.notes: The service documents a simple HTTP upload API with a curl example.

security.notes: The service runs over HTTPS, but the cited public pages do not claim end-to-end encryption.

sources.label: Homepage
sources.notes: Current upload page says files are auto-deleted after 60 minutes and capped at 100 MB.

sources.label: API
sources.notes: Current API page documents a POST upload endpoint and a curl example, and repeats the 60-minute automatic deletion rule.

allowed_file_types.notes: The official upload page and API accept a generic file parameter with a 100 MB cap and 60-minute retention. No extension allowlist is published on the cited pages.

public_sharing.notes: Public link or share-page support is part of this verified file-sharing record; see the cited source notes for the current sharing surface.

name: 0x0.st
summary: Minimal temporary file host with 512 MiB uploads, HTTP POST and remote URL upload support, and formula-based retention from 30 days to 1 year.

free_model.notes: This published host has a verified free, guest, or free-account path in the dataset. Recheck first-party sources if the service moves to trial-only or paid-only access.

max_file_size.notes: The official homepage says the maximum file size is 512.0 MiB.

retention.notes: Retention is conditional. The official page says files are valid for at least 30 days and up to 1 year, with lifetime calculated from file size.

storage.notes: 0x0.st is a temporary upload-and-share host, not a reusable personal storage account.

bandwidth.notes: Bandwidth is conditional. The official FAQ publishes historical outgoing traffic of 10-40 TB/month but no per-user quota.

account.benefits: No account is required. Upload responses include an X-Token header for delete/expiry management operations.

developer.notes: The homepage documents multipart HTTP POST upload, remote URL upload, retention fields, management-token operations, and cURL examples.

allowed_file_types.notes: The public terms prohibit abuse categories, but the upload API is general file-oriented rather than limited to one media type.

public_sharing.notes: The service creates public share/download links for uploaded files.

security.notes: The service runs over HTTPS and publishes privacy/moderation notes, but it is not an end-to-end encrypted host.

sources.label: 0x0.st homepage
sources.notes: Official homepage verified max_size 512.0 MiB, min_age 30 days, max_age 1 year, multipart POST upload, remote URL upload, cURL examples, and public terms.

sources.label: Rentry limited file-host list
sources.notes: Seed list claimed 512 MB for 0x0.st; official page confirms 512.0 MiB.

name: 1Cloudfile
summary: Freemium file host with free/non-account uploads up to 10 GB, 20-day free retention, broad file-type support, and account-key API documentation.

free_model.notes: The FAQ says uploading and downloading are 100% free for all users, with premium accounts adding flexibility.

max_file_size.notes: The official FAQ says free/non-account and premium uploads must be less than 10 GB per file.

max_file_size_guest.notes: The official FAQ says free/non-account uploads must be less than 10 GB per file.

max_file_size_account.notes: The official FAQ and premium page both say account/premium uploads are capped at 10 GB per file.

retention.notes: The official FAQ says free/non-account files and premium files are kept for 20 days, despite the homepage also using broader forever-style marketing language.

storage.notes: The cited public pages do not publish a clean numeric free storage quota.

bandwidth.notes: Bandwidth is conditional. The premium page advertises direct downloads, maximum speeds, and no limit on the amount of downloads for premium, but the cited pages do not publish a numeric free transfer quota.

account.benefits: Free/non-account uploads are described in the FAQ. Registering adds an account area and API-key access.

developer.notes: Official API docs cover account authorization, file upload/info, folder creation/listing, and SSL-required API requests; they require an account/API key and do not publish a first-party cURL example.

allowed_file_types.notes: The official FAQ says allowed file types are Any, subject to terms and copyright rules.

public_sharing.notes: The homepage describes pre-generated HTML code, website linking, and social sharing for uploaded files.

security.notes: The API docs require SSL for API requests, but the cited pages do not claim end-to-end encryption.

sources.label: 1Cloudfile homepage
sources.notes: Official homepage describes free file upload, sharing tools, account registration, file management, and API navigation.

sources.label: 1Cloudfile FAQ
sources.notes: Official FAQ says uploading/downloading is free, free/non-account files are kept for 20 days, allowed file types are Any, and free/non-account uploads must be less than 10 GB.

sources.label: 1Cloudfile premium page
sources.notes: Official premium page says premium gets direct downloads, no ads, unlimited simultaneous downloads, max download speeds, files kept for 20 days, uploads up to 10 GB, download-manager support, and no limits on number of downloads.

sources.label: 1Cloudfile API docs
sources.notes: Official API docs describe account/API-key credentials, SSL-required API host, authorization, file upload/info, folder operations, and API response fields.

sources.label: PolyUploader supported-hosts table
sources.notes: Seed table listed 1Cloudfile as 5 GB / 30 days; current official FAQ now says less than 10 GB and 20-day retention.

name: 1FileSharing
summary: Timed file-hosting service with guest uploads, 1 GB free uploads, and premium upgrades for larger files and longer retention.

free_model.notes: This published host has a verified free, guest, or free-account path in the dataset. Recheck first-party sources if the service moves to trial-only or paid-only access.

max_file_size.notes: The current upload page states a 1.00 GB maximum file size for free uploads.

max_file_size_account.notes: Maximum upload size is conditional for account holders. The current public pages say paid accounts get larger uploads than the 1 GB free guest flow, but they do not publish one clean non-premium signed-in cap in accessible text.

retention.notes: Retention depends on download activity. Current public pages describe free files as sharable for 30 days, while also saying free-member files may be removed after 7 days without downloads.

storage.notes: Storage is conditional. The public upload flow does not publish a standalone guest storage quota, while the current download and premium comparison pages advertise unlimited storage for premium accounts and inactivity-based cleanup for free uploads.

bandwidth.notes: Bandwidth varies by plan. The download page says free downloads run at 10 Mbps with a 60-second wait, while premium downloads run at maximum speed and the premium comparison says there are no limits on the amount of downloads.

account.benefits: Guest uploads work without an account; paid accounts add faster downloads, larger uploads, and longer file retention.

developer.notes: The cited public pages do not document a public API or CLI upload workflow.

security.notes: The cited public pages do not claim end-to-end encryption for uploads.

sources.label: Upload page
sources.notes: Current upload flow, 1 GB free upload limit, and retention wording for free uploads.

sources.label: Download page
sources.notes: Current free vs premium comparison including waiting time, speed, and 30-day vs 180-day file retention wording.

allowed_file_types.notes: 1FileSharing's official upload page presents a general file-upload flow with a 1 GB free upload limit and retention rules. No narrow extension allowlist or blocklist is published on the cited pages.

public_sharing.notes: Public link or share-page support is part of this verified file-sharing record; see the cited source notes for the current sharing surface.

name: 4shared
summary: Long-running cloud storage and file-sharing service with a free 15 GB tier, 2 GB max uploads, and official mobile apps.

free_model.notes: This published host has a verified free, guest, or free-account path in the dataset. Recheck first-party sources if the service moves to trial-only or paid-only access.

max_file_size.notes: The official features page currently lists a 2048 MB maximum upload file size for the free plan.

retention.notes: Retention depends on account activity. 4shared says free-account files remain available as long as you keep logging in at least once every 180 days; if you do not log in for 180 days, the account files are deleted, with popular files sometimes kept longer.

storage.notes: The official features page currently lists 15 GB of storage space for the free plan.

bandwidth.notes: Unlimited bandwidth volume within account-space limits. The official help page says there are no restrictions on the amount of data that can be transferred, as long as uploads and downloads stay within the size of your account space.

account.benefits: A verified 4shared account is required to access essential features such as sharing, previewing, and downloading files and folders; login is also required to download files larger than 500 MB.

developer.notes: The cited public pages do not document a public developer API or CLI workflow.

security.notes: The cited public pages do not claim end-to-end encryption for the free plan.

allowed_file_types.notes: 4shared says you can upload any files that meet its Terms of Use and publishes popular shareable examples across document, music, video, image, and archive formats. Illegal, pornographic, or copyrighted materials are prohibited.

name: Akira Box
summary: Freemium file-sharing host with guest uploads, 5 GB free guest files, 20 GB free registered uploads, and longer retention for account users.

free_model.notes: This published host has a verified free, guest, or free-account path in the dataset. Recheck first-party sources if the service moves to trial-only or paid-only access.

max_file_size.notes: Akira Box currently markets a 5 GB maximum upload size for visitor uploads on both the homepage and premium comparison page.

max_file_size_guest.notes: Visitors are currently limited to 5 GB per file.

max_file_size_account.notes: Registered free users are currently limited to 20 GB per file.

retention.notes: Retention is conditional. Visitor files are deleted 7 days after the last download, while registered free-user files are deleted 30 days after the last download. Premium files are not deleted while shared.

storage.notes: Unlimited storage is currently advertised for both visitor and registered free tiers.

bandwidth.notes: Unlimited. A current official Akira Box article says the service imposes no sharing restrictions on downloads, recipients, or bandwidth.

account.benefits: Guest sessions allow quick uploads. Standard accounts use an email sign-in link and raise the free max upload size to 20 GB with longer retention.

developer.notes: Akira Box publishes an API page, but the upload API is explicitly premium-only, so it is not counted as a practical free-tier API in this dataset.

security.notes: The privacy page says services are provided in encrypted form and recommends users encrypt their own content for maximum privacy, but the public pages do not claim end-to-end encryption.

name: Android File Host
summary: Large Android-focused file hosting network for developers with unlimited downloads, multiple mirrors, and a public beta API.

free_model.notes: This published host has a verified free, guest, or free-account path in the dataset. Recheck first-party sources if the service moves to trial-only or paid-only access.

max_file_size.notes: Max file size is conditional. Android File Host does not publish a formal general per-file upload cap for the free tier, but current public file listings include downloads up to at least 1.6 GB, so practical upload size clearly exceeds smaller ROM-sized files and may vary by device or project listing.

retention.notes: Retention depends on download activity. The terms say Android File Host generally will not delete uploaded files, but it may remove files with fewer than 10 downloads over 365 consecutive days.

storage.notes: Storage is conditional. Android File Host says it does not limit the number of files, but the terms still reserve the right to suspend service for excessive storage use, so no clean byte quota is published.

bandwidth.notes: Android File Host markets unlimited bandwidth and download speeds in its "Unlimited Everything" section, while the terms still reserve the right to suspend service for excessive bandwidth use.

account.benefits: Developers need an account for uploads and account-managed services; visitors can still browse device pages and download public files without registering.

developer.notes: Android File Host publishes a beta API for browsing devices, developers, OTA update checks, folders, and files; direct-download URLs require a beta API key from the user dashboard.

security.notes: The site runs over HTTPS and provides hosted storage, but the public pages do not claim end-to-end encryption or promise that stored files are encrypted for you.

name: Backblaze B2
summary: Object storage service with a 10 GB free tier, public APIs, and official iPhone and Android access.

free_model.notes: This published host has a verified free, guest, or free-account path in the dataset. Recheck first-party sources if the service moves to trial-only or paid-only access.

max_file_size.notes: Backblaze's large-file guide says B2 large files can range from 5 MB to 10 TB, which is the current documented upper bound for account-based uploads.

retention.notes: No automatic expiry by default. Backblaze's B2 lifecycle documentation says B2 keeps uploaded files and file versions until the user deletes them or configures lifecycle rules to hide or delete older versions.

storage.notes: Backblaze B2 includes the first 10 GB of total storage for free.

bandwidth.notes: Backblaze B2 includes the first 1 GB of downloads per day for free.

account.benefits: A Backblaze account is required to enable B2, create buckets, manage files, and use the web or mobile apps.

developer.notes: Backblaze publishes B2 Native and S3-compatible API documentation, and its cloud storage docs also link to command-line interface resources.

security.notes: Backblaze B2's Native API docs say only HTTPS requests are supported. The cited public pages do not claim end-to-end encryption for stored files.

name: Disroot Upload

free_model.notes: This published host has a verified free, guest, or free-account path in the dataset. Recheck first-party sources if the service moves to trial-only or paid-only access.

The current upload page lets users choose 24 hours, 7 days, 30 days, or 90 days, but the actual expiry becomes the smaller of the chosen delay and the instance's size-based server limits: 0-96 MB files are kept 90 days, 96-954 MB files are kept 15 days, and 954 MB or larger files are kept 2 days.

name: Drime
summary: European cloud storage and collaboration platform with 20 GB free storage, secure sharing, an official API, and an optional end-to-end encrypted Vault space.

free_model.notes: This published host has a verified free, guest, or free-account path in the dataset. Recheck first-party sources if the service moves to trial-only or paid-only access.

max_file_size.notes: The accessible current free-plan pages do not publish a separate lower per-file cap. Since the free plan includes 20 GB of storage, the practical free upload ceiling cannot exceed 20 GB.

retention.notes: Retention is conditional. Drime says the free plan has no time restriction on the use of free space during normal use, but the Terms allow suspension or termination after 12 consecutive months of inactivity.

storage.notes: The homepage and pricing pages say the free plan includes 20 GB of storage.

bandwidth.notes: The pricing page lists the free plan with unlimited bandwidth.

account.benefits: A Drime account is required for cloud storage, collaboration, API tokens, and CLI access, while shared files can be sent to collaborators without accounts.

developer.notes: Drime publishes a public developer API page, API reference, and CLI install command, and says API calls are free.

security.notes: Drime says the main service uses AES-256 encryption and EU hosting, with an optional Vault area for client-side end-to-end encryption.

name: Dropbox
summary: Mainstream cloud storage service with a free 2 GB Basic plan, official desktop and mobile apps, and a well-documented developer API.

free_model.notes: This published host has a verified free, guest, or free-account path in the dataset. Recheck first-party sources if the service moves to trial-only or paid-only access.

max_file_size.notes: For Dropbox Basic, the practical free upload ceiling is the 2 GB free storage quota, even though Dropbox as a platform supports much larger uploads on paid plans.

retention.notes: Dropbox does not publish an automatic expiry for files kept in storage; files remain until the user deletes them, after which deleted files are recoverable for a plan-dependent window.

storage.notes: Dropbox Basic currently offers 2 GB of free storage.

bandwidth.notes: Bandwidth varies by sharing mode. Dropbox says sharing activity can be interrupted for exceeding bandwidth limits or for links that are downloaded too many times in one day, but it does not publish one clean Basic-plan transfer quota.

max_file_size_account.notes: For Dropbox Basic, the practical free upload ceiling is the 2 GB free storage quota.

account.benefits: A Dropbox account is required to use the Basic free plan, sync files, and access desktop and mobile apps.

developer.notes: Dropbox provides official developer documentation and APIs for file storage and sharing integrations.

security.notes: Dropbox publicly documents encryption and secure sharing, but the cited Basic-plan pages do not present the service as end-to-end encrypted by default.

retention.notes: OneDrive does not publish an automatic expiry for files kept in storage; files remain until the user deletes them, after which deleted files stay in the personal Recycle Bin for 30 days.

storage.notes: Microsoft's free OneDrive tier currently lists 5 GB of cloud storage.

security.notes: The cited free-plan pages mention file encryption and ransomware protection, but they do not present OneDrive personal storage as end-to-end encrypted by default.

name: put.re
summary: Anonymous file-sharing service with browser uploads, direct links, optional albums, and a public API.

free_model.notes: This published host has a verified free, guest, or free-account path in the dataset. Recheck first-party sources if the service moves to trial-only or paid-only access.

max_file_size.notes: The official features page says the web interface supports uploads up to 200 MB, while the documented HTTP API examples describe a 100 MB API limit.

retention.notes: Files remain until deleted by the uploader or removed for terms-of-service violations.

storage.notes: Storage is conditional. put.re exposes anonymous uploads and optional album-style management rather than a published reusable storage allowance, and its official feature pages only promise that files remain until deleted or removed for terms violations.

bandwidth.notes: Bandwidth is conditional. put.re does not publish numeric transfer quotas, and the official feature pages only promise CDN-backed delivery, direct links, and fast access rather than a fixed bandwidth allowance.

max_file_size_guest.notes: Anonymous browser uploads work without an account and are capped at 200 MB. The uploader source switches from plain HTTP to WebSockets above 50 MB.

storage_guest.notes: Storage is conditional. Anonymous uploads are supported and can optionally be organized through album-style management flows, but the official pages do not publish a reusable guest-storage quota.

account.benefits: No account is required. Uploads return delete links, and the public API also supports album creation with an ID and secret for later editing.

developer.notes: The public API docs include curl examples for file upload, deletion, metadata lookup, album creation, health checks, and removal requests.

allowed_file_types.notes: The features page says there is no file type restriction. Unknown types are served as application/octet-stream, and active content such as HTML or JavaScript is forced to text/plain to prevent browser execution.

public_sharing.notes: Public link or share-page support is part of this verified file-sharing record; see the cited source notes for the current sharing surface.

security.notes: The features page says files are stored encrypted in Amazon S3, never written to disk unencrypted, and are served over HTTPS. The service does not claim end-to-end encryption.

name: Yandex Disk
summary: Cloud storage service with a 5 GB base tier, official desktop and mobile apps, and a Linux console client.

free_model.notes: This published host has a verified free, guest, or free-account path in the dataset. Recheck first-party sources if the service moves to trial-only or paid-only access.

max_file_size.notes: Without an active Yandex 360 plan, the maximum size of a single uploaded file is currently 1 GB.

retention.notes: Files remain until deleted. Yandex says files are stored until you delete them if there are no access issues, with 30 days of Trash recovery, while permanent deletion can still happen after 2 years of not using Yandex Disk or after a space-overage block persists for 90 days.

storage.notes: Current public help pages use 5 GB as the base Yandex Disk capacity in their examples.

bandwidth.notes: Yandex limits uploads to twice the storage capacity of the current plan each month. With the 5 GB base plan, that works out to 10 GB of uploads per month before uploads are temporarily restricted.

account.benefits: A Yandex account is required to use Yandex Disk storage, sharing, desktop sync, and mobile apps.

developer.notes: The cited current public pages describe official apps and a Linux console client, but they do not document a public developer API.

security.notes: The cited help pages do not claim end-to-end encryption for standard Yandex Disk storage.

sources.label: Storage Space Help
sources.notes: Current help examples show 5 GB base capacity and a monthly upload limit of twice storage capacity.

sources.label: Desktop Help
sources.notes: Windows and macOS desktop apps plus Linux console client.

sources.label: Mobile Help
sources.notes: Android and iOS app availability.

sources.label: Upload Help
sources.notes: Current 1 GB single-file upload limit without a paid plan.

sources.label: Security help
sources.notes: Official security help stating files are stored until deleted when there are no access issues, with 30 days of Trash recovery and specific long-term deletion edge cases.

sources.label: Upload files and folders
sources.notes: Official Yandex Disk help says users can upload files and folders, with plan-based file-size caps and antivirus checks.

allowed_file_types.notes: Yandex Disk's official help says users can upload files and folders; problems are framed around file size, storage capacity, antivirus scanning, permissions, folder nesting, and names rather than a narrow extension allowlist.

public_sharing.notes: Public link or share-page support is part of this verified file-sharing record; see the cited source notes for the current sharing surface.

name: Your File Store
summary: Simple no-account file upload service with private download links, optional password protection, and long-lived files that stay active with periodic access.

free_model.notes: This published host has a verified free, guest, or free-account path in the dataset. Recheck first-party sources if the service moves to trial-only or paid-only access.

max_file_size.notes: The current official upload page and FAQ both state files can be up to 500 MB in size.

retention.notes: Files remain available indefinitely as long as they are accessed at least once every 60 days.

storage.notes: Storage is conditional. The cited public pages do not publish a total storage quota, and file availability instead depends on the 60-day activity rule rather than a reusable account-space allowance.

bandwidth.notes: Bandwidth is conditional. Your File Store does not publish a numeric transfer cap, and the current official download pages insert a setup/wait step before delivering the file and explicitly ask users to support the free service through adverts.

max_file_size_guest.notes: The current official upload page and FAQ both state guest uploads can be up to 500 MB in size.

account.benefits: No membership or signup is required to upload and share files.

developer.notes: The cited public pages do not document a public API or command-line upload workflow.

security.notes: The service uses private download links and optional password protection, but the cited public pages do not claim end-to-end encryption.

sources.label: Upload Page
sources.notes: Current service description, 500 MB limit, optional password protection, and private-link behavior.

sources.label: FAQ
sources.notes: Current 500 MB limit and 60-day inactivity retention policy.

sources.label: About
sources.notes: Current no-signup statement and advertising-supported service description.

sources.label: Download page
sources.notes: Current official download page showing a setup/wait step before download delivery and ad-supported free access.

allowed_file_types.notes: Your File Store's FAQ says users can store any type of file except scripts that could accidentally run on the server, giving examples such as .pl, .php, .phps, .py, .htaccess, and .htc. The site recommends archiving blocked scripts first.

public_sharing.notes: Public link or share-page support is part of this verified file-sharing record; see the cited source notes for the current sharing surface.

name: YourImageShare
summary: Image and video hosting platform with anonymous uploads, free accounts for more control, CDN-backed sharing, and a 100 MB total-per-upload-batch limit.

free_model.notes: Official indexed pages describe a free media-sharing platform with anonymous uploads.

max_file_size.notes: The official indexed FAQ says the maximum size of all uploads in one batch should be under 100 MB.

retention.notes: Conditional: the official FAQ guarantees anonymous uploads for 6 months, after which retention depends on request activity; registered-user uploads persist until the uploader deletes them unless rules are violated.

storage.notes: Official indexed FAQ text describes free, unlimited storage space, but not a numeric per-account quota.

bandwidth.notes: Conditional: YourImageShare does not publish a numeric free bandwidth quota; official indexed text describes CDN/distributed file storage for fast media delivery, while the FAQ/moderation rules allow content removal or account suspension for spam or rules violations.

max_file_size_guest.notes: The official indexed FAQ says the maximum size of all uploads in one batch should be under 100 MB.

max_file_size_account.notes: The official indexed FAQ says the maximum size of all uploads in one batch should be under 100 MB; registered accounts add management rather than a higher published free batch cap.

account.benefits: Anonymous uploads are described as available; free accounts unlock more control and customization.

developer.notes: The cited official pages do not document a public API or first-party CLI workflow.

security.notes: The service runs over HTTPS and describes secure sharing, but does not claim end-to-end encrypted hosting.

sources.label: YourImageShare homepage
sources.notes: Official homepage/search-indexed text describes a free image and video hosting platform for uploading, hosting, and sharing visual content.

sources.label: YourImageShare contact FAQ
sources.notes: Official indexed FAQ verifies 100 MB per upload batch, free storage, supported formats, and retention rules for anonymous and registered uploads.

sources.label: YourImageShare about/FAQ
sources.notes: Official about/FAQ search-indexed text says anonymous users can upload without an account and free accounts unlock advanced features.

sources.label: FMHY Image Hosts
sources.notes: Seed list claimed 100 MB / forever for YourImageShare; official indexed FAQ confirms a 100 MB batch-size limit and free/unlimited storage language.

allowed_file_types.notes: The official FAQ lists JPEG, JPG, PNG, GIF, MP4, and WEBM.

public_sharing.notes: Official pages describe direct links, embeddable tags, and sharing hosted images/videos.

name: Zero Upload
summary: Free/premium file host with free uploads up to 5 GB, 70-day free retention, public API documentation, and account-based file management.

free_model.notes: This published host has a verified free, guest, or free-account path in the dataset. Recheck first-party sources if the service moves to trial-only or paid-only access.

max_file_size.notes: The official FAQ says each free/non-account upload must be less than 5 GB.

retention.notes: The official FAQ says free/non-account files are kept for 70 days.

storage.notes: Storage is conditional. The homepage says users can store and manage data, but the cited public pages do not publish a total free storage quota.

bandwidth.notes: Bandwidth is conditional. Premium removes waiting and direct-download restrictions; the cited pages do not publish a numeric free bandwidth cap.

max_file_size_guest.notes: The official FAQ says free/non-account uploads must be less than 5 GB.

max_file_size_account.notes: The official FAQ says free/non-account uploads must be less than 5 GB; premium accounts raise the cap to 11 GB.

account.benefits: Free upload/download is available. API and file-management operations require account authentication / API keys.

developer.notes: Official API documentation covers authenticated file upload, download, info, edit, delete, folder, and account endpoints over HTTPS.

allowed_file_types.notes: The official FAQ says any file type may be uploaded, subject to terms.

public_sharing.notes: The service creates public share/download links for uploaded files.

security.notes: The public pages run over HTTPS and expose API auth, but do not claim end-to-end encryption.

sources.label: Zero Upload FAQ
sources.notes: Official FAQ says uploading/downloading is free, free/non-account files are kept for 70 days, any file type is allowed, and free/non-account files must be less than 5 GB.

sources.label: Zero Upload API docs
sources.notes: Official API docs describe HTTPS API host, account authentication, file upload/download/info/edit/delete, folder operations, and account package fields.

sources.label: Zero Upload homepage
sources.notes: Official homepage advertises free upload/share/manage flows with premium no-wait direct-download upgrades.

sources.label: Rentry limited file-host list
sources.notes: Seed list claimed 57.32 MB for Zero Upload, but current official FAQ says 5 GB for free/non accounts; the dataset uses the first-party value.

name: ZeroFS
summary: No-account file storage/share service with no published size limit, unlimited active-file storage, 15-day inactivity retention, link-private sharing, encrypted-at-rest files, ID-based optional accounts, and an empty API docs page.

free_model.notes: The homepage exposes direct uploads without a paid gate and says no account is required.

max_file_size.notes: The homepage says there are no size limits, and the FAQ says the file size limit is none.

retention.notes: The homepage says 15-day retention on free plans, and the FAQ says inactive files are deleted after 15 days.

storage.notes: The FAQ says storage is unlimited for active files; this is conditional on activity and does not imply permanent storage.

bandwidth.notes: The cited pages do not publish a numeric bandwidth or download quota.

account.benefits: No account is required, but an ID-based account can keep track of files; the FAQ warns the ID cannot be recovered if lost.

developer.notes: The site has an API Documentation page, but the fetched page contains only the heading and no usable endpoint or CLI details.

allowed_file_types.notes: The terms prohibit unlawful, harmful, copyright-infringing, child-exploitation, terrorist, doxxing, extreme-violence, malware, and malicious-software content; no extension allowlist is published.

public_sharing.notes: Files are private unless the user shares the link; terms say access is determined by possession of the corresponding difficult-to-predict link.

security.notes: The homepage says files are encrypted at rest, and the privacy policy says stored files use industry-standard encryption with keys managed within the system; no cited page claims E2EE.

sources.label: ZeroFS homepage
sources.notes: Official homepage verifies no-account uploads, no size limits, private-unless-link-shared positioning, encrypted-at-rest claim, region selector, folder upload, chunk-size options, and 15-day free-plan retention.

sources.label: ZeroFS FAQ
sources.notes: Official FAQ verifies no account requirement, no file size limit, unlimited active-file storage, 15-day inactivity deletion, and link-private sharing.

sources.label: ZeroFS API docs
sources.notes: Official API page exists but currently publishes only an API Documentation heading and no usable endpoint details.

sources.label: ZeroFS privacy policy
sources.notes: Official privacy policy verifies no collected name/email/phone, account ID model, no persistent IP logs, encrypted-at-rest storage, and no behavioral tracking cookies beyond ad providers.

sources.label: ZeroFS terms
sources.notes: Official terms verify prohibited content categories, link-based sharing access, content removal policy, and no persistence warranty.
